reasoning: The <style> tag is present in the wp_kses_wf() allowlist (lines 467-472), which is accurate. However, the finding fails on the reachability prong. All identified callers of wp_kses_wf() pass developer-controlled PHP string literals, not attacker-controlled input: utility.php (checked(), create_toggle_switch(), create_select_options(), create_radio_group()) builds $out from hardcoded HTML templates; admin.php line 128 uses $notice['text'] populated only by add_notice() calls with PHP string literals; functions.php line 786 uses $display_message set to hardcoded error strings; setup.php passes phpversion() and get_bloginfo(); tab_design.php uses $template_id from a hardcoded PHP array returned by get_templates(). No code path was found where attacker-controlled data containing a <style> tag reaches wp_kses_wf(). Additionally, all call sites are within the wp-admin interface (admin_menu hooks, admin notices) requiring administrator privilege, so even if a path existed the privilege bar is high. The <style> allowlist is an overly broad sanitizer configuration but not an exploitable XSS vector given the absence of any attacker-controlled input reaching the function. The also-present <script> allowlist (lines 501-507) is a separate, potentially higher-severity concern not claimed here.
